chunks.title: Course Information
chunks.content:
Data Visualization. (3-0). Credit 3. Visual representation and design of data and information; 3D visualization, infographics, data narratives, principles of visual data encoding, and interaction techniques. Prerequisites: Graduate classification or approval of instructor. Cross-listed with VIZA 676.

This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the field of data visualization and visual analytics, guiding students through both foundational concepts and advanced techniques in visual representation. Beginning with an overview of data types and visual analytic tasks, students will explore key frameworks, including the Nested Model of Visual Analytics and principles of marks and channels. Advanced topics covered include multivariate, sequential, spatial, network, and text visualization, and the integration of multiple views for more complex datasets. Innovative areas like data storytelling, augmented and virtual reality (AR/VR) for data, and the role of data visualization in Explainable AI, as well as e-learning, are also discussed, offering students a forward-thinking perspective on the field. Through hands-on tutorials in D3 and Vega-Lite and later in web programming, students will gain practical skills for crafting interactive visualizations. Students will present project pitches, receive feedback, and collaborate on projects, culminating in final presentations.

Special Course Designation
None

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Graduate classification or approval of instructor.
Cross Listing: VIZA 676/CSCE 679.
There will be assignments and projects that involve programming in HTML/CSS/JavaScript for the UI part, and Python for data and AI. While having programming experience in both is great, it is not required.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
At the end of the course, students will be able to:
• Analyze diverse data types (e.g., categorical, quantitative, temporal, spatial, network, and text data) and map visual analytical tasks to appropriate visual marks and encoding channels.
• Compare, select, and justify visualization techniques for multivariate, sequential, spatial, network, and text data based on data characteristics, analytical goals, and perceptual effectiveness.
• Apply the Nested Model for Visualization Design and established Rules of Thumb to design, implement, and refine visual analytic systems.
• Evaluate state-of-the-art data visualization applications in domains such as data storytelling, immersive analytics (AR/VR), explainable AI, e-learning, and digital twins, using design principles and empirical evidence.
• Design and develop an end-to-end data visualization project on a topic of the student's choice, including problem formulation, data preparation, visualization design, implementation, and evaluation.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Recommended
Visualization Analysis and Design
Authors: Tamara Munzner

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• Students are required to complete a course project over the course of the semester. Undergraduate students may choose any tools or platforms for implementation. Graduate students must implement their project using the D3.js visualization library: 50%
• Reading responses (students are expected to read papers and give their comments on papers assigned): 20%
• Assignments (students are expected to submit a series of assignments, which they must do individually; these assignments are about analyzing and visualizing data): 20%
• Class participation: 10%
• The grading scale will be:
• A = 90-100
• B = 80-89
• C = 70-79
• D = 60-69
• F = <60

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
You'll lose 10% for each late day. Submissions will be accepted until three days after the deadline. After that, you'll get 0 on that assignment.
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempted from the late work policy (Student Rule 7). The student is responsible for informing the instructor in a timely manner about excused absences. The instructor will then work with the student to catch up on their submissions, including missed group class participation.
